Title: NAD+ deficiency primes defense metabolism via 1O2-escalated jasmonate biosynthesis in plants

Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: anti-insect defense via glucosinolate turnover products
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Overexpression of BGLU28 in qs-2 depletes glucosinolates and suppresses enhanced insect resistance without rescuing the small-growth phenotype
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: 35S overexpression in qs-2; glucosinolate profiling; insect feeding/preference assays
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful for testing or modulating glucosinolate turnover; however reducing glucosinolates compromises insect resistance
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. BGLU28 overexpression hydrolyzes glucosinolates in qs-2. 2. Reduced glucosinolate abundance eliminates enhanced insect resistance. 3. Growth defects persist, separating defense metabolites from the small-plant phenotype.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Used as a functional depletion tool to show glucosinolates cause resistance phenotype.

Common_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: respiratory burst oxidase homolog; NADPH oxidase
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Mutation of RBOHF removes H2O2 and O2− accumulation in qs-2 but does not reduce jasmonate or glucosinolate hyperaccumulation, showing these ROS are dispensable for defense activation
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: analysis of qs-2rbohF3 double mutant; ROS staining; targeted metabolite profiling
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Distinguishes ROS branches; manipulating RBOHF alone is unlikely to block NAD-deficiency-induced JA defense priming
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. RBOHF mediates accumulation of H2O2 and O2− in qs-2. 2. Removing these ROS does not affect JA pathway activation or glucosinolate accumulation. 3. Therefore chloroplastic 1O2, not RBOHF-derived ROS, is the relevant trigger upstream of JA biosynthesis.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Included because it is directly tested in Results as a mechanistic discriminator, but it is not a positive driver of the focal nutrient-metabolism outcome.

Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Transient expression of HsSARM1-TIR depletes NAD+ and increases 1O2, JA and JA-Ile in tobacco leaves, recapitulating the NAD-deficiency defense sequence
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: transient expression in N. benthamiana; NAD+/cADPR quantification; 1O2 and JA/JA-Ile measurements
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: also hydrolyzes NADP to a lesser extent
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Serves as a heterologous tool to induce NAD depletion and defense priming experimentally; not directly a breeding target due to likely growth/metabolic penalties
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. HsSARM1-TIR expression reduces NAD+ and produces cADPR in N. benthamiana. 2. Mild NAD+ depletion is sufficient to trigger 1O2, JA, and JA-Ile accumulation. 3. This supports causal linkage between NAD+ depletion and defense metabolism activation across species.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Heterologous mammalian protein used for mechanistic recapitulation rather than native plant pathway control.

Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: defense against insect attack; growth-defense metabolic balance
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: forms quinolinate, a precursor in de novo NAD+ biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: QS deficiency lowers NAD+ and primes jasmonate-dependent defense metabolism, causing hyperaccumulation of glucosinolates and enhanced insect resistance, with reduced growth
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: qs-2 mutant analysis; genetic complementation (COM1/COM2); nicotinic acid rescue; metabolite profiling; RNA-seq; targeted metabolomics; insect feeding assay
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: growth retardation; reduced chlorophyll; impaired photosynthetic efficiency
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: NAD homeostasis is a metabolic checkpoint that could be tuned to enhance biotic stress resilience, but constitutive deficiency causes growth penalty
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: key upstream control point for NAD homeostasis, not established as pathway rate-limiting enzyme
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Mutation of QS caused NAD+ deficiency in qs-2. 2. QS deficiency induced JA-dependent metabolic reprogramming and hyperaccumulation of glucosinolates in rosette leaves. 3. Complementation or nicotinic acid rescue restored NAD+, reduced defense metabolites, and alleviated growth defects.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: QS is the initiating causal gene for NAD+ deficiency in the study; its metabolic effect on glucosinolate accumulation is indirect through 1O2 and JA biosynthesis escalation.
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: normal soil growth; nicotinic acid supplementation; insect infestation

Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: activates defense metabolism and promotes insect resistance through downstream glucosinolate accumulation
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: LOX2 is the prominent 13-LOX driving α-LeA consumption, JA production, and downstream glucosinolate accumulation in qs-2 and during insect attack
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: qRT-PCR; immunoblotting; CRISPR knockout in Col-0 and qs-2; targeted quantification of α-LeA, 13-HPOT, 12-OPDA, JA, JA-Ile and glucosinolates; DCMU treatment; insect infestation response
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: strongly expressed in rosette leaves; upregulated in qs-2 and upon insect infestation
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: enhanced defense metabolism is associated with growth inhibition when activated in qs-2 background
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: LOX2 is a target to modulate inducible JA-mediated defense, but constitutive activation may impose growth costs
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: supported as key control point/prominent 13-LOX in rosette leaves for JA induction
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. LOX2 transcript and protein accumulate strongly in qs-2. 2. lox2 knockout blocks α-LeA conversion to 13-HPOT, lowers JA pathway intermediates, and restores glucosinolate levels in qs-2 toward wild type. 3. LOX2 is also the major 13-LOX induced during insect attack and required for robust JA accumulation.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: LOX2-mediated step is escalated by chloroplastic singlet oxygen rather than H2O2/O2−.

Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: supports defense metabolism and insect resistance through JA biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: converts fatty acid hydroperoxide intermediate toward allene oxide en route to OPDA and JA
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Disruption of AOS in qs-2 suppresses JA accumulation, reduces glucosinolates, restores growth, and lowers insect resistance
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: CRISPR-Cas9 knockout of AOS in qs-2; qRT-PCR of JA marker genes; JA/JA-Ile and glucosinolate quantification; growth and insect assays
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: blocking JA biosynthesis reduces defense and insect resistance but improves growth in qs-2
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: AOS is useful to tune defense-growth tradeoffs by modulating JA biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. AOS is required for elevated JA signaling in qs-2. 2. qs-2aos restores glucosinolate levels close to wild type. 3. Blocking AOS alleviates growth defects and defense phenotypes in qs-2.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: AOS is used as functional pathway validation for JA dependence rather than as the central mechanistic focus.

Regulation_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: F-box jasmonate receptor component of SCF complex
Regulation_Genes.Regulation_Mode: jasmonate perception and transcriptional activation of JA-responsive programs; positive feedback on JA biosynthetic genes
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: JA-responsive genes THI2.1; PDF1.2; VSP1; JA biosynthetic genes including LOX2 and other JA pathway genes
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: activates JA-responsive and JA-biosynthetic gene expression
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: jasmonate signal transduction and positive feedback amplification of defense metabolism
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Loss of COI1 suppresses JA signaling, reduces glucosinolate accumulation, restores growth, and weakens insect resistance in qs-2
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: essential JA signaling component required to translate NAD+ deficiency into defense metabolite accumulation
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: double mutant qs-2coi1-2; qRT-PCR of marker genes; hormone and glucosinolate profiling; growth and insect feeding assays
Regulation_Genes.Feedback_or_Feedforward_Regulation: positive feedback on JA biosynthesis is supported because coi1 mutation downregulates JA biosynthetic genes and lowers pathway intermediates
Regulation_Genes.Upstream_Signals_or_Inputs: jasmonate/JA-Ile; NAD+ deficiency acts upstream of JA signaling
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: loss of defense activation reduces insect resistance while improving growth in qs-2
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: JA signaling nodes like COI1 can rebalance growth and defense, but inhibition compromises pest resistance
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. COI1-dependent JA signaling is activated in qs-2. 2. coi1 mutation suppresses JA markers, glucosinolate accumulation, and insect resistance in qs-2. 3. COI1 also sustains positive feedback on JA biosynthesis genes.
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: COI1 acts downstream of NAD+ deficiency and 1O2 accumulation; ROS accumulation is independent of COI1.

Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: CRISPR disruption of MYB28 together with MYB29 in qs-2 abolishes aliphatic glucosinolate accumulation and eliminates enhanced insect resistance
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: major regulator of aliphatic glucosinolate branch required for the defense phenotype in qs-2
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: CRISPR-Cas9 triple mutant qs-2myb2829; glucosinolate profiling; insect feeding assay
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. MYB28 together with MYB29 is required for aliphatic glucosinolate accumulation in qs-2. 2. Loss of aliphatic glucosinolates abolishes qs-2 insect resistance. 3. This demonstrates aliphatic glucosinolates are the dominant defense metabolites in this system.
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Can be used to manipulate aliphatic glucosinolate-mediated insect defense with branch specificity.
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Used mainly to dissect which glucosinolate class confers resistance rather than upstream NAD-JA signaling.

Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Together with MYB28, required for aliphatic glucosinolate accumulation and qs-2 insect resistance
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: branch regulator for aliphatic glucosinolate-dependent defense phenotype
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: CRISPR-Cas9 triple mutant qs-2myb2829; glucosinolate profiling; insect assay
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. MYB29 acts with MYB28 to control aliphatic glucosinolate accumulation. 2. Removal of this branch suppresses insect resistance in qs-2.
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful for branch-selective engineering of aliphatic glucosinolate defense.
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Function inferred jointly with MYB28 from combinatorial mutant.

Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: defense metabolite, but less dominant than aliphatic glucosinolates for H. armigera resistance here
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Together with MYB51, its disruption removes indolic glucosinolates in qs-2 but does not abolish insect resistance
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: branch regulator for indolic glucosinolate accumulation, but not decisive for the observed insect resistance phenotype
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: CRISPR-Cas9 triple mutant qs-2myb3451; glucosinolate profiling; insect assay
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. MYB34 together with MYB51 controls indolic glucosinolate accumulation in qs-2. 2. Loss of indolic glucosinolates does not eliminate qs-2 insect resistance, indicating a lesser contribution than aliphatic glucosinolates.
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Allows branch-specific manipulation of indolic glucosinolates without strongly altering the tested insect resistance phenotype.
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Used as genetic dissection of metabolite class contribution.

Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: defense metabolite, secondary contribution in this insect assay system
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Together with MYB34, required for indolic glucosinolate accumulation, but not essential for qs-2-mediated insect resistance
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: important for indolic glucosinolate branch, not decisive for the focal resistance phenotype
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: CRISPR-Cas9 triple mutant qs-2myb3451; glucosinolate profiling; insect assay
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. MYB51 works with MYB34 to regulate indolic glucosinolate accumulation in qs-2. 2. Indolic glucosinolate loss does not remove enhanced resistance to H. armigera.
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential target for branch-specific engineering of indolic glucosinolate chemistry.
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Function inferred jointly with MYB34 from combinatorial mutant.
